aliases: Unagi Rice Bowl with Egg and Miso Soup

english: Unagi Rice Bowl with Egg and Miso Soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for unagi don / eel rice bowl combo. Mapped to the broader 'rice dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for unagi don. A00GJ (rice-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible broader category in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for unagi don or eel rice bowl combo. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: sweet soy-based glaze (tare sauce with mirin and sugar)
miso soup (miso paste, dashi broth, tofu, wakame seaweed)

serving.description: 1 set meal (unagi bowl, half-boiled egg, miso soup or drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal comprising a grilled eel rice bowl, one half-boiled egg, and a choice of miso soup or drink, weighing approximately 600 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175130) and Cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756), combined with HPB Singapore nutrient data for miso soup and egg components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for an unagi bowl combo as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled eel (FDC ID 175130, ~30% by weight), cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756, ~45% by weight), half-boiled egg (FDC ID 173424, ~10% by weight), and miso soup (HPB Singapore, ~15% by weight). The input record's per-100g values (energy 141.67 kcal, protein 7.5 g, fat 5.83 g, carbohydrate 15.0 g) are consistent with this weighted blend and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 300 mg/100g reflects the combined contribution of tare sauce and miso paste and is plausible. All values are documented as estimated.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for an unagi bowl combo. The GI of 72 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: cooked white rice (jasmine/short-grain), which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The rice component constitutes approximately 45% of the dish by weight and is the primary glycaemic driver. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (72) × carbohydrate per serving (90 g from 600 g × 15 g/100g) / 100 = 64.8. This is a high GL meal.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 300 mg/100g (1800 mg per 600 g serving) from tare sauce, soy sauce, and miso paste approaches the daily recommended limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce in the tare glaze, ask for miso soup on the side to control portion, and avoid adding extra soy sauce at the table to keep total sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice contributes a high glycaemic index (~72) and a glycaemic load of approximately 64.8 per serving; the tare sauce adds mirin and sugar, further elevating post-meal glucose response, while dietary fibre is low at 0.67 g/100g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request brown rice or reduce the rice portion to lower glycaemic load, ask for tare sauce on the side to limit added sugar, and pair the meal with additional non-starchy vegetables to moderate post-meal blood glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Freshwater eel provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) that support cardiovascular health, but sodium at 300 mg/100g (1800 mg per serving) and cholesterol at 41.67 mg/100g (250 mg per serving) require monitoring for individuals with established CVD.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease can benefit from the omega-3 content of unagi but should request reduced-sodium preparations, limit the tare sauce to reduce saturated fat and sugar intake, and monitor overall sodium and cholesterol contribution within their daily dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Freshwater eel (unagi) contains omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) with anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat is low at 1.67 g/100g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory omega-3 content of unagi; however, they should request reduced-sodium preparations to minimise sodium-driven tissue swelling and consider brown rice to reduce glycaemic-driven inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Unagi Bowl Combo (うなぎ丼コンボ; Unagi Don Combo) is a Japanese set meal served in Singapore featuring grilled freshwater eel (unagi) glazed with a sweet soy-based tare sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, and sugar, served over a bed of steamed white rice. The combo includes a half-boiled egg and a choice of miso soup or a drink. The eel is prepared by grilling over charcoal or a gas grill and repeatedly basting with tare sauce, producing a caramelised, lacquered exterior with a tender, rich interior. The flavour profile is savoury-sweet with umami depth from the eel and tare, mild starchiness from the rice, and a silky, slightly runny yolk from the half-boiled egg. Miso soup adds a warm, fermented umami note with tofu and wakame seaweed. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 141.67 kcal per 100g, with 7.5 g protein, 5.83 g fat, and 15 g carbohydrate. Per 600 g serving, this equates to approximately 850 kcal, 45 g protein, 35 g fat, and 90 g carbohydrate. The eel is a notable source of omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA), vitamin A, and vitamin B12. Sodium is elevated at 300 mg/100g (1800 mg per serving) due to tare sauce and miso paste. The glycaemic index is high (~72) driven by white rice, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets. It is a popular lunch and dinner option in Japanese restaurants and food courts across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in unagi don set?
Is unagi bowl good for diabetes?
Is unagi rice bowl high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for an unagi bowl combo as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled eel, cooked white rice, half-boiled egg, and HPB Singapore miso soup data. The input values are consistent with this blend and are retained. GI is estimated from the dominant white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite unagi bowl combo are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2024. Individual component data exists but composite dish-level values for these fields are not published in an approved source. trans_fat_g is 0.02 g/100g, a trace level consistent with the eel and egg components. No core macros are zero; all four core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) are non-zero and supported by the composite source estimation. The dish is not halal-certified as mirin (rice wine) is used in the tare sauce. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it is not an approved primary source; USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore were used instead.
